AI will handle routine translation. What it won't handle is this:



The moment a frustrated client switches from English to Spanish mid-call, and your rep follows without missing a beat.

The contract negotiation where the subtext matters more than the words.

The sales pitch where understanding the cultural frame changes the whole close.

HSBC lost $10 million on a rebranding disaster because machine translation missed cultural nuance. Amazon's Swedish launch failed for the same reason.

Bilingual isn't a language skill. 

It's a cultural intelligence skill. 

And no AI model has cracked that.

Companies hiring bilingual LATAM professionals aren't protecting against AI. 

They're accessing something AI can't replicate.

What's a moment where cultural intelligence changed the outcome of something at your company?

Hello job seekers—and hiring managers. Hope everyone is having a great week.

I’ve been searching for my next opportunity since my last contract ended in January, and I wanted to share a few observations (which may sound familiar to many here) and ask for some advice.

Back in 2022, when I was job hunting, I was getting so many interviews that I had to build a spreadsheet just to track them all—and I landed a new role within three months. Roughly one out of every three applications turned into an interview.

Now, if even 1% of my applications led to a response, I’d consider that a win.

I’ve tried everything—tailored resumes, customized cover letters, and multiple AI tools—but I’m still struggling to get in front of any hiring teams.

I’ve also been contacted by several independent recruiters who seem to really like my resume, but those conversations never turn into interviews with their clients.

I imagine many of you are experiencing something similar. If anyone has found strategies that are actually working in today’s market, I’d really appreciate you sharing.

Thank you!

Started this week in London, and what a week it's been already. 

First, time with Lena Chauhan talking #AI, #governance, and #podcasts. Lena built a career in finance and risk before turning her attention to something more urgent: helping organisations, educators, and families navigate AI ethically and effectively. She also hosts Hardwired: The Human Algorithm, a podcast on what it means to be human in a world of intelligent machines. It's become essential listening for me.

Later, over to the Hive Founders demo day. Eight women-led startups pitching, all actively raising, every one of them doing real work:

💰 Welleness, presented by Lucy Smith. A financial wellness app for women who are doing everything right and still feel financially vulnerable. Personalised insights, an AI confidante called Elle, and a path to regulated female advisers when you're ready for one.
🍐 Pear, showcased by Dr Jo Carlile. A therapist matching platform. Warm, jargon-free, and designed for the moment you know something isn't right but have no idea where to start. Huge thanks to Tallie Hopkins, who told me about the accelerator when we first met.
🦉 Hirehoot, introduced by Ilknur Tas Ergul. Confidential, AI-powered hiring for senior sales, marketing and growth roles at fast-growing tech companies. Pre-verified candidates and discreet outreach for leadership positions that usually leak before they're filled.
🐕 Loop Pet Food, presented virtually by Laura Stanford. Circular, insect-powered pet food built on a proper sustainability thesis. Black soldier fly protein, rescued vegetables from smallholder farmers, and a bag of kibble actually earning its planet-friendly label.
🤖 NoHarm, introduced by Delma Ramirez. Procurement intelligence for climate and critical technologies. Structured verification and due diligence for enterprises deploying innovation at scale, where getting the supplier right genuinely matters.
👩‍⚕️ Fig Health, showcased by Aurelia Clavien. Fixing systemic bias in women's health by pulling fragmented health data into one complete picture, so women get diagnosed faster and with the full context.
⚖️ Juris AI, demo'd by Meera Ahmadi. AI contract review built by lawyers, for in-house teams in recruitment and property. Takes contract review from two hours to ten minutes, with actual legal intelligence underneath rather than general-purpose guesswork.
🤰 Mothercore, introduced by Ksenia Fortin-Brazeau. The UK's first virtual pelvic floor physiotherapy platform, built on the simple truth that leaking, heaviness and pelvic pain after pregnancy or through menopause are common, but not normal. 1:1 online sessions with vetted experts.

Beyond the pitches, it was great to meet and talk to these women. Real belly laughs, moments of connection, and inspiration. So many more exciting chats to come!

Huge thanks to the incredible team at Hive. It was great to meet Andrea Sommer, Alexandra Florea and Sarah Crudgington. I'm excited to take part in more events.

We are looking for experienced engineers with deep design expertise to create challenging, simulation-based design problems for training and evaluating advanced AI models. The role focuses on crafting realistic engineering design tasks where a model must make genuine trade-offs, size components, and iterate against physics-based simulators to meet competing specifications.

• Open Positions: 60
• Work Mode: Fully Remote

• Design and validate simulation-based engineering problems

• Ensure real-world constraints and optimization challenges
• Collaborate with researchers to refine AI model performance
🔬 Domain Tracks

🎓 Required Qualifications

10 things I tell clients to land elite US law firms with NQ's of £175,000

᠎
𝟭. 𝗦𝘁𝗼𝗽 𝘁𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗨𝗦 𝗳𝗶𝗿𝗺𝘀 𝗹𝗶𝗸𝗲 𝗠𝗮𝗴𝗶𝗰 𝗖𝗶𝗿𝗰𝗹𝗲 𝗳𝗶𝗿𝗺𝘀
᠎
White & Case, Kirkland & Ellis, Ropes & Gray, and Sidley Austin are not bigger Magic Circle firms. Smaller intakes. Deeper written screens. Partner-heavy interviews.
᠎
𝟮. 𝗧𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝗶𝘀 𝗻𝗼 𝗖𝗩 𝘀𝗰𝗿𝗲𝗲𝗻 𝗮𝘁 𝗦𝘁𝗮𝗴𝗲 𝟭
᠎
White & Case, Ropes & Gray, and Sidley Austin do not use a CV screen at Stage 1. Written answers are the gateway.
᠎
Jack proved it. Low A-levels. Non-target university. White & Case London training contract. £175,000 NQ salary.
᠎
𝟯. 𝗘𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆 "𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗳𝗶𝗿𝗺" 𝘀𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲 𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗱𝘀 𝗼𝗻𝗲 𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗳𝗶𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝗳𝗮𝗰𝘁
᠎
The PEAL-X™ rule: named practice group, named landmark deal, named Chambers Band 1 ranking.
᠎
"I admire your culture" fails at every US elite firm.
᠎
𝟰. 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗱 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗺𝗲𝗿𝗰𝗶𝗮𝗹 𝗳𝗹𝘂𝗲𝗻𝗰𝘆, 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗴𝗿𝗮𝗱𝗲𝘀
᠎
Commercial Fluency™ at workstream level separates offers from rejections. Why Kirkland & Ellis dominates private equity. Why Ropes & Gray leads on fund formation. Why Sidley Austin ranks top in restructuring.
᠎
𝟱. 𝗥𝗲𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗖𝗩 𝗮𝘀 𝗲𝘃𝗶𝗱𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲, 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗱𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗲𝘀
᠎
"Assisted with research" is not a bullet. "Researched 14 jurisdictions on conflicting choice-of-law provisions, reducing partner review time by 40%" is a bullet.
᠎
That is the VTMR™ standard: Value, Transferability, Metrics, Relevance.
᠎
𝟲. 𝗧𝗲𝗰𝗵𝗻𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹 𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗺𝘀 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝘃𝗲 𝗳𝗹𝘂𝗲𝗻𝗰𝘆
᠎
Strong PEAL-3™ answers reference transaction structures, governing law clauses, dispute resolution mechanisms, and regulatory frameworks. Weak answers reference "interesting work."
᠎
𝟳. 𝗦𝗧𝗔𝗥-𝟯® 𝗶𝘀 𝗻𝗼𝗻-𝗻𝗲𝗴𝗼𝘁𝗶𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗲𝘁𝗲𝗻𝗰𝘆 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝗲𝘄𝘀
᠎
Three categorised Actions. Three categorised Results. Each closing with a Link to a specific function at the firm. This is what survives three levels of partner follow-up.
᠎
𝟴. 𝗣𝗮𝗿𝘁𝗻𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝘀𝗰𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝗼𝗻 𝗽𝗿𝗲𝗰𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻, 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗽𝗮𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻
᠎
Generic enthusiasm does not differentiate at US elite firms. Specificity does.
᠎
𝟵. 𝗨𝘀𝗲 𝗔𝗜 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗿𝗲𝗽𝘀. 𝗡𝗼𝘁 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗼𝗳𝗳𝗲𝗿𝘀.
᠎
AI can tell you what STAR stands for. It cannot tell you what the partner at Kirkland & Ellis wrote on the scorecard when they rejected 400 people before lunch.
᠎
AI coaches have zero documented offers at White & Case, Ropes & Gray, or Sidley Austin. We have the letters on file.
᠎

Former recruiter. 10,000+ applications reviewed from the hiring side of Goldman Sachs, Blackstone, White & Case, and the Magic Circle.
᠎
100+ documented offers across London, New York, and Hong Kong.
᠎
UCL. MBA student club sessions at Harvard Business School, Yale School of Management, and MIT Sloan.
᠎
♻️ Repost if this helps someone targeting a US law firm training contract this cycle.
᠎

I just read through 68 profiles from our waitlist.

Here are the top 10 LinkedIn profile mistakes I saw:

1. Bad profile pictures.

Selfies. Cropped group shots. No smile. Grainy photos from 5+ years ago.

Animated images.

A bad headshot tanks your callback rate before a recruiter reads a single word.

2. Jobs listed in months, not years.

"6 mo" at four different companies looks like you can't hold a job.

If it was contract, label it Contract. If it was a layoff, say so in the description.

No doubt some real circumstances prompted you to leave, we have all been fired, but this impacts your marketability to those who spend 30sec on your profile. 

3. Fourteen years at one company, one title.

A single logo for a whole career reads as loyal to you and one-trick to a hiring manager.

Break it into the real roles, promotions, and scope changes you actually had.

4. Unreadable names.

"A (KN) Chen" and names with three aliases, bracketed initials, and emojis all lose the search.

Pick the name a recruiter will type into a search bar. Use only that one.

5. A locked-down profile.

If I can't see your headline, your experience, or send you a connection request, I'm skipping you. So is every recruiter.

Open the profile. Turn on Open to Connections.

6. Your college is your "Current Role."

When your Experience section is empty, LinkedIn quietly shows University of Maryland (graduated 2007) as where you work today.

Add a consultancy under your initials, an advisory seat, or a Career Break entry. Anything current.

Example: AS Design or AS Advisory or AS Consulting

7. Zero info on your tech stack.

"Senior Software Engineer at Amazon" tells me nothing.

Frontend? Backend? Python, Go, Swift? Say it. Recruiters search for keywords.

8. Six year career gaps with no explanation.

Silence on a gap is not neutral. It reads as a red flag.

One sentence in the Experience section fixes it. Caregiving. Sabbatical. Health. Startup that worked but then didn't. Say what happened.

9. Four sections that all say the same thing.

Your About, your headline, your Featured, and your top skill all say "strategic leader driving transformation."

Pick one story. Tell it once, well.

10. Recommendations from 2009.

A recommendation from 16 years ago makes you look frozen in time.

Ask two current colleagues this month. Return the favor.

Bonus: your LinkedIn is a landing page, not a resume.

If the first five seconds don't answer what you do, where, and for whom, rewrite the top of the profile.

Thanks for your continued support!

Follow along Alex Shinkarovsky & Reverse Recruiting Agency

Who Actually Writes Federal IT Job Descriptions

Earlier in this series I examined how commercial technology requirements find their way into TS/SCI job descriptions and how the FAR's procurement structure shapes the staffing requirements that eventually reach the cleared talent market. There is a more granular version of that story worth telling.

Who actually writes a federal IT job description?

The answer, in most cases, is not the engineer who will do the work. It is not the technical lead who understands the program's operational environment. It is not someone who has spent time inside a SCIF thinking carefully about what skills the mission actually requires.

The typical workflow looks something like this.

A capture manager identifies an opportunity. A proposal team assembles. Labor categories are selected from existing contract vehicles — GSA schedules, GWACs, existing BPA structures — because those categories already have approved descriptions and price ceilings that fit the bid. A proposal writer, working under deadline pressure, builds the staffing section from a combination of the labor category definitions, the solicitation's PWS language, and whatever the competitive landscape suggests the government wants to see.

At some point, someone searches for current industry benchmarks to validate the technical requirements. Those benchmarks come from commercial sources — job boards, industry salary surveys, competitor proposals. Kubernetes is dominant in commercial cloud. It goes in. Python is the language of AI and data engineering. It goes in. The certifications that appear most frequently in commercial job postings for similar titles get added to the preferred qualifications.

The result reaches HR, who builds a screening checklist from the proposal's staffing requirements. That checklist becomes the filter applied to every resume submitted against the posted position.

At no point in that workflow did anyone ask whether a cleared professional working in an air-gapped environment with a restricted toolchain could realistically have developed the skills now appearing on the checklist. At no point did anyone model the actual cleared talent pool against the requirements being set. At no point did the operational reality of the classified environment inform the document that will determine who gets an interview.

This is not a story about bad actors. Every person in that workflow is doing their job as it has been defined for them. The problem is that the workflow itself was never designed to produce job descriptions that accurately reflect the cleared environment. It was designed to produce proposals that win contracts.

Those are different objectives. And the cleared talent market lives with the consequences of that difference every day.

We need to be talking more about the kind of fear people are feeling around AI. 

With Anthropic release of Mythos it's made me realise we have no control over our own fear anymore. 

I don't think it's fair to say Anthropic is just using 'savvy marketing' to announce their new model, this ignores the deeper problem. 

Society has been drip fed fear for the last five years, to the point where we can no longer recognise a genuine threat over a manufactured one.

To me, there are two kinds of fear.

Productive fear is attached to something specific. It is the fear that makes you read a contract, question who built the tool that just made a decision about you. It is grounded, and it points at harm in a way you can actually act on.

Manufactured fear is the vast, abstract kind. It is shaped for you, usually by someone with something to gain from the shape it takes. It concentrates attention on a future that may or may not arrive, while pulling attention away from a present that is already here.

Three years of extinction headlines have trained us in the second kind. 

Meanwhile there are real threats going on every single day which we could actually use our productive fear to solve. 

Three years of extinction headlines have trained us in the second kind.

Meanwhile, there are real threats going on every single day that we could actually use our productive fear to solve.
 
Hiring algorithms being sued for age discrimination. AI video interview tools rejecting deaf applicants. Job ad algorithms ruled discriminatory by European regulators. 

Named people. Documented cases. Happening now.

These are the harms we have the actual power to push back on, and they are the ones getting the least attention.

The question is not whether to be afraid. It is whether the fear you are carrying is the fear that you chose and would actually help you. 

I do not think we get our agency back by being less afraid. I think we get it back by getting better at knowing what to be afraid of, and why.

#AIEthics #ResponsibleAI #AILiteracy #DigitalRights #FutureOfWork

Meta didn’t acquire the AI startup.

They absorbed its brain.

Meta has hired multiple founding members and senior engineers from Mira Murati’s company. Not peripheral roles—core builders, system architects, the people who actually know how these systems are designed and run.

This isn’t a hiring story.

It’s a governance problem.

AI capability doesn’t sit inside companies. It moves with the people who build it.

No acquisition occurred. No diligence. No governance review. But the knowledge layer transferred anyway.

That exposes the gap.

Governance frameworks are built around entities: companies, vendors, systems, contracts. They assume capability is contained within those structures.

It isn’t.

In AI, capability lives in design decisions, training methods, system intuition, and operational judgment. All of that moves with individuals.

So when those individuals move:

Who owns the system behavior they shaped?
Who is accountable for embedded design decisions?
Who carries the risk when those decisions surface elsewhere?

There is no clean chain.

Because the governance model doesn’t account for movement at that layer.

What you get instead is a system that can scale and transfer capability faster than it can:

– trace origin
– track evolution
– assign accountability

That’s the gap.

Not policy. Not intent. Not awareness.

Governance is built for static ownership. AI capability moves dynamically through people.

So the structure shows control, while the actual authority moves outside it.

This isn’t a talent war.

It’s a visibility failure.

By the time you see where the capability went, it’s already been applied somewhere else.

And governance still thinks the system is where it used to be.

Written in collaboration with ChatGPT``

2 years.
That's how long Dario Amodei — CEO of the most safety-obsessed AI lab on the planet — says we have until AI matches all human cognitive work.
Not Altman. Not Musk. Not the loudest voice in the room.
The one whose entire business model is built on caution. The one who has spent years WARNING about AI risk.
When the pilot who never panics tells you to brace, you don't argue whether the plane is really shaking.
Yet most leaders are still debating whether AI is "real."
Here's what Dario put on the record at Davos 2026 and in his "Adolescence of Technology" essay:
→ AGI within 2 years
→ 50% of entry-level white-collar jobs displaced in 1–5 years
→ Most software engineering work replaceable in 6–12 months
→ AI is now designing its own successors ("endogenous acceleration")
If he's wrong, you lose nothing by preparing.
If he's right, you lose everything by waiting.
So what does "preparing" actually look like?
→ Audit which roles in your organization AI eats first. Be brutally honest.
→ Stop hiring for tasks that will be automated in 18 months. Hire for judgment, accountability, and system design.
→ Retrain your workforce now — not after the layoffs.
→ Build AI fluency at the BOARD level. You cannot lead through this if you don't use the tools yourself.
→ Rewrite the social contract with your employees before reality rewrites it for you.
The companies that survive the next 24 months will not be the ones with the best AI strategy on a slide deck.
They'll be the ones whose leaders stopped debating and started moving.

Two deadlines. Twelve weeks. Most organizations are not ready.

Colorado AI Act enforces June 1. EU AI Act high-risk obligations hit August 2.

This is not a 2027 problem. It's a Q2 problem. And the companies most exposed are not the ones you'd expect.

Mid-size US firms. EU clients. AI running in hiring, lending, customer scoring, or contract review. And a working assumption that the EU AI Act belongs to someone else.

It doesn't.

If your AI touches EU residents, EU obligations follow. Jurisdiction tracks impact, not incorporation. The "we're too small to matter" assumption has an expiration date. That date is August 2.

Here's what audit-ready actually requires for each.

𝗖𝗼𝗹𝗼𝗿𝗮𝗱𝗼 𝗔𝗜 𝗔𝗰𝘁 - 𝗝𝘂𝗻𝗲 𝟭
A live AI inventory. Not a spreadsheet from Q3. Every system touching employment, credit, housing, education, or healthcare, with owner, use case, data inputs, and decision impact documented.

A bias impact assessment. Completed and on record. Colorado requires demonstrable reasonable care. Assumed reasonable care won't hold up.

A public risk disclosure for affected systems. It needs to exist before a complaint, not because of one.

An appeal mechanism for automated decisions. If your system influences something consequential, the person on the receiving end needs a real path to human review.

𝗘𝗨 𝗔𝗜 𝗔𝗰𝘁 - 𝗔𝘂𝗴𝘂𝘀𝘁 𝟮
A conformity assessment. Not scheduled. Done.

A technical file covering architecture, training data governance, performance metrics, and oversight mechanisms. Auditors will ask mid-engagement. Build it now.

A human oversight mechanism that actually functions. Someone identified, trained, and empowered to intervene or shut the system down. A process on paper is not a person with authority.

Post-market monitoring running. The EU AI Act treats deployment as the beginning of governance. Logging, incident tracking, and performance review are obligations.

Twelve weeks sounds like runway. It isn't.

A conformity assessment takes six to ten weeks from scratch. A bias audit on a production system runs three to five. An AI inventory across an organization with shadow AI in every department takes longer than anyone budgets.

The organizations that scramble in July are deciding right now that they have time.

They don't. Neither do you.

Ladies and gentlemen, let me introduce you to Mark.

A Senior Product Manager (one of the most in-demand roles in Berlin). Six years of experience. Strong companies on his CV. His contract ends at a mid-sized SaaS company, and he takes a few weeks to reset. Fair.

Then he starts applying again.

His CV is packed: “led cross-functional teams,” “owned product strategy,” “improved user engagement.” Sounds solid. It also sounds like everyone else. No sharp signal, no clear positioning.

Silence.

He assumes it’s the market. Maybe he needs to upskill. So he does a Product Analytics course, adds some AI certification. Still nothing.

Now the doubt kicks in. Maybe he’s overqualified. Maybe he should aim lower. Maybe change direction. Time goes by, money starts to matter, confidence drops.

Here’s what’s actually happening:

Recruiters are not reading his CV the way he thinks. They scan in seconds. They look for alignment, relevance, and proof. Not effort. Not potential. Not intentions. If the role says “hands-on product execution,” and Mark looks strategic, he’s out. If the role is senior, but his achievements aren’t clearly tied to impact and scale, he’s out. If the story doesn’t make immediate sense, they move on.

That gap, between how candidates see themselves and how the market reads them, is positioning. Most candidates never fix it. They react instead: More applications, more courses, more frustration.

A good coach helps you see the gap. Sharpens the narrative. Forces clarity.
Market Signal is that coach.

Market Signal breaks down how your profile is actually perceived under hiring pressure, flags credibility gaps, and shows where your positioning is working against you. Then it rebuilds your CV, LinkedIn, and direction around roles you can realistically land, not just roles you want.

This the closest thing to having a hiring manager tell you the truth before you waste months guessing.

If you’ve been getting silence and it doesn’t add up, you’re probably closer to Mark than you think.

Ireland’s Q1 Job Surge: 16% Growth, but the "Easy Hire" is Over

The data is in from the latest Morgan McKinley  via Laura Varley at Silicon Republic Knowledge & Events Management Ltd, and the numbers tell a fascinating story of a "disciplined" market.
While professional job openings in Ireland rose by 16.2% in Q1 2026 compared to the end of last year, we aren't seeing a return to the "hiring frenzy" of previous cycles. Instead, we are entering a phase of high scrutiny, skills led recruitment.

Key Takeaways:
Selectivity is the New Standard: Even with a double-digit increase in roles, employers are more cautious. Hiring processes are lengthening as companies prioritise "long-term fit" and "immediate capability" over rapid headcount expansion.

The AI Baseline: AI skills are no longer a "nice to have" or a niche advantage. The report highlights that AI capability is becoming a baseline expectation across multiple professional disciplines.

A Shift in Power: With job seeker activity up by 36% quarter on quarter, the balance is tilting back towards employers. This is reflected in more restrained salary movements and stricter in office requirements, particularly within the Dublin tech and financial hubs.

The Contract Weight: In sectors like Life Sciences and Technology, there is a heavy lean towards contract hiring to maintain flexibility while delivering on critical, time bound projects.

My Take: At TalentHunter, we are seeing this "disciplined" approach first hand. It is no longer enough to just have the right title, you need to demonstrate how you deliver on transformation and risk management from day one. 
For employers, the challenge isn't finding candidates, it’s navigating the increased volume to find the specific, high-impact talent that fits a more rigid set of requirements

Are you seeing a longer "time-to-hire" in your sector, or is the 16% surge making things feel more fluid?

a lot of engineering roles right now aren't coming with a perfectly defined JD.

they come with a problem, a stack, and the expectation that you'll figure the rest out.

I'm hiring a Senior Full Stack Product Engineer for a client. remote, 3-6 months contract with potential to go permanent.

here's what the role actually looks like day to day:

you get a feature request that's half-baked. maybe a few sentences from a stakeholder or a UI wireframe. 

you turn that into architecture, build it, ship it. 

Next.js and TypeScript are the core of the stack. you're writing APIs, wiring up databases, building frontend components, debugging across every layer.

and you're doing all of this with AI baked into how you work. not as a novelty. as the default. agents handling QA, automating chunks of your pipeline, accelerating how you deliver. 

if you're still writing every test by hand and manually reviewing every PR line by line.. you're probably not gonna enjoy this.

the work itself is interesting though. we're talking about products being used by governments and large institutions. stuff that actually matters at scale.

must-haves:
- 5+ years shipping production web apps (if you don't have 5 years don't be discouraged, reach out)
- Next.js and React (you've already built with this)
- TypeScript, REST APIs, relational databases
- comfortable owning features end to end
- AI tools are part of your actual workflow, not something you "experimented with"

nice to haves: .NET, Azure, Playwright, LLM integrations
if you're the kind of engineer who thrives with ambiguity and autonomy and gets frustrated by structure for the sake of structure.. this one's for you.

DM me if interested. or tag someone who'd be a fit.

yes I know "AI-native" is becoming one of those phrases people throw around. but in this case they actually mean it. i promise.

repost, comment, share with a friend or any engagement would be massively appreciated!

As a contract recruiter, give me a scope of work over a job description every time! 
 
I filled a role with a new client last week – best of all, they called me!

I pride myself on sourcing top quality candidates, usually from my existing network, in minimal time. On this occasion the role was slightly outside of my core market, so I had to qualify candidates not already in my network, but I was still able to find the perfect candidate in less than 24 hours.  

I was only able to do this because the hiring manager shared the scope of work for the project with me, rather than a job description. 
This allowed me to:
-         Refine my search to find profiles with very specific experience.
-         Clearly articulate what the project would be and what would be required from the contractor.
-         Qualify candidates on when they had delivered similar projects, rather than what key skills they have. 
-         Get candidates excited about the opportunity.

I’d love to hear the thoughts of other contract recruiters, experienced contractors or hiring managers.

🚨 POV: $2T Shift No One Is Calling Out Clearly.

This is not digital transformation. This is not AI-led growth.
This is CONTROL moving back to enterprises.

What’s actually happening beneath the noise: Across AI, GCC expansion, and vendor compression, we’re seeing a potential:

$1.5T–$2T redistribution of control

Not new value.
Reassigned value.

Breakdown:

• ~$600B → AI-driven productivity (30–40% effort collapse)
• ~$500B → GCC insourcing (3–4x hiring vs services in key markets)
• ~$400B → Platform consolidation (fewer vendors, deeper lock-in)
• ~$300B → Services compression (pricing + scope reset)

Structural shift (most are missing this):
- CONTROL → Enterprises (AI, data, IP, decisioning)
- PLATFORM → Product ecosystems (owning workflows)
- EXECUTION → Services (integration, orchestration, AI layering)

Services are no longer “builders”, they are becoming system orchestrators.

Hard signals already visible in live environments:
• Large deals fragmenting: $150M–$200M programs → $40M–$80M modular contracts

• Core engineering quietly moving in-house: 60–70% shift in strategic areas

• Pricing pressure compounding: 20–30% drop + 30% effort reduction from AI

That’s not optimization. That’s a structural margin reset.

Truth:

“Enterprises didn’t lose capability. They outsourced it. Now with AI they’re buying it back. Faster.”

What happens next (next 3–5 years):
• Enterprises will own AI, data, and core IP layers
• GCCs will become innovation engines—not cost centers
• Services firms will compete on:
→ Speed
→ Integration depth
→ AI orchestration capability

Not headcount.

Take: This isn’t a cycle. This is a once-in-a-decade control reset.

And it’s already showing up in:
* Deal pipelines
* Hiring patterns
* Vendor consolidation decisions

Question (for leaders actually in it):

Are you already seeing:
→ AI reducing deal sizes?
→ More work pulled into GCCs?
→ Clients reclaiming core engineering?

Or is this still “future talk” in your world?

The scariest tech metric right now isn't an AI benchmark.

It's 177.

Vaibhav Sisinty dropped this number on Raj Shamani's podcast — and I had to rewind it twice.

In the last 12 months, the top 5 IT firms in India — TCS, Infosys, Wipro, HCL, and Tech Mahindra — had a combined net headcount addition of 177 employees.

Not 1,77,000. Not 17,700. 177.

These same companies were hiring lakhs every single year not long ago. The engine hasn't slowed. It's been structurally dismantled.

---

A trillion dollars was wiped off global IT stocks — not from a recession — but because investors did one calculation: if AI agents handle finance, ops, marketing, and support, you don't need the headcount.

Salesforce Agentforce. ServiceNow AI. Already in enterprise contracts.

---

The Labour Arbitrage model is structurally finished. Revenue kept growing. Headcount collapsed. That decoupling is the whole story.

The era of the Safe Employee is over. The only asset that compounds in an AI-first economy is Leverage.

---

You don't have to found a company. But you have to become a Builder.

A Builder doesn't execute tasks — they build systems that do.
A Builder doesn't sell hours — they sell outcomes.

As Vaibhav said: "Becoming an employee was the most stable thing. Building something of your own — right now — is the most stable thing."

That's not inspiration. That's survival architecture.

---

Two camps are forming: those waiting for the hiring cycle to return, and those who've accepted it isn't.

Which camp are you building from?

↓ And the real question: Is this a structural collapse of the labour arbitrage model — or a cyclical dip dressed in AI clothing?

Drop your read. I'll engage with every serious take.

I've talked a lot about how macro instability - particularly geopolitical tension and technological change - has been a tailwind for fractional and interim hiring, pushing companies toward optionality over commitment. That's the demand side. But there's an equally significant force reshaping the supply side: AI.

Fractional execs who've genuinely embedded AI into the way they operate are becoming meaningfully more productive. 

This shows up in two ways:

1. Delivery to clients

A fractional CFO who used to spend days building a board pack or financial model can now produce one in hours. A fractional COO can synthesise operational data across a client's tools and surface a diagnostic in the first week, not month. 

The ramp-up time that used to constrain interim engagements is compressing fast, and challenging the common push back: "how deep can they really go?".

2. Internal admin and ops

Most fractional execs are essentially solopreneurs, and the admin stack that comes with managing multiple clients - proposals, contracts, scheduling, invoicing, knowledge management - used to eat a serious chunk of productive time. AI has significantly alleviated this burden.

The net effect is that the fractional & interim operators leveraging AI effectively can serve more clients at a higher standard.

They bring the strategic judgement and context that no tool replaces, and they've offloaded the execution and administrative drag that used to cap what one person could take on.

With both demand rising and supply getting stronger, the fractional model is structurally improving.

Contract hiring in DevOps is no longer a backup plan.
It is becoming the first choice for delivery.

Over the past 6 to 12 months, hiring has shifted from building teams to solving problems. Budgets are tighter, timelines are shorter, and there is less room for slow hiring processes.

That is where contract comes in.

When the scope is clear, bringing in someone for a defined piece of work is often faster and more effective than trying to hire permanently. 

Whether it is a cloud migration, a platform rebuild, or an AI proof of concept, the focus is on outcomes rather than headcount.

It is a different way of thinking about hiring, but it is one that a lot of teams are leaning into.

A Product Manager walks into a sprint review and says, “We need speed, flexibility, and zero long-term commitments.” HR nods…and hires contractors.

That mindset took me back to 2015, when I was a Northeastern University intern at an insurance product company in the U.S. I walked into the IT team expecting a traditional setup—only to find nearly 25 people, most of them contractors. It felt less like a plug-and-play engine built for delivery.

Back then, contractors were the PM’s secret weapon—need a data pipeline? Call a contractor. Dashboard by Friday? Contractor. Production bug at 2 AM? Definitely a contractor.

But here’s the twist—cloud + AI just rewrote the playbook. With platforms like Google Cloud Platform and Amazon Web Services, teams can build reusable, automated systems instead of repeatedly hiring for the same tasks. Add agentic AI, and suddenly your “contractor” is an AI agent that doesn’t need onboarding or coffee breaks ☕.

AI comment: My AI agent asked for a promotion…turns out it already automated my job description.

So, do we end contract culture? Not fully—but we reduce dependency by building smarter, cloud-native, AI-driven teams.

In today’s evolving IT landscape, layoffs have created uncertainty—but they’ve also pushed organizations to rethink how they build and scale teams.

One of the smartest shifts I’m seeing is the increased adoption of staff augmentation and contract-based resources.

Here’s why this model is becoming a game-changer:

🔹 Cost Efficiency
Instead of long-term fixed costs, companies can align expenses directly with project needs. No overheads of full-time hiring, onboarding, or bench costs.

🔹 Faster Time-to-Market
Need a skilled resource quickly? Contract professionals can be onboarded in days, not months—helping teams accelerate delivery timelines.

🔹 Access to Specialized Skills
Whether it’s cloud migration, AI/ML, or legacy modernization, you can bring in niche expertise exactly when required.

🔹 Scalability & Flexibility
Scale your team up or down based on workload. This agility is critical in uncertain market conditions.

🔹 Reduced Risk
Short-term engagements allow organizations to evaluate talent and adapt strategies without long-term commitments.

In a time where efficiency, speed, and adaptability define success, staff augmentation is not just a cost-saving tactic—it’s a strategic advantage.
Companies that embrace this model will not only survive market fluctuations but also build more resilient and future-ready teams.

Paralegal Supply Is Pricing This Wrong

There are currently 47,000 open paralegal and legal assistant postings on LinkedIn in the United States. That number has barely moved since January. What has moved is the number of applicants per posting: up roughly 340 percent over the same period, according to LinkedIn's own hiring-trend data published in March. The ratio broke something in the usual equilibrium. Law firms are not hiring faster. Candidates are not finding work faster. The postings are becoming a kind of fiction. The underlying mechanism is not mysterious. Harvey, the legal AI platform backed by OpenAI, closed a $300 million Series D in February and announced expanded enterprise deployments across Am Law 100 firms including Latham & Watkins and A&O Shearman — relationships those firms have confirmed in press statements. The pitch to managing partners is straightforward: one Harvey seat handles document review, contract analysis, and first-draft memo work at a volume that previously required three to five junior associates or a small paralegal pool. The firms are not firing existing staff en masse; they are simply not backfilling. Non-backfilling is the invisible attrition. It...

Did a little Q1 review of where in the business AI is moving...

331 GenAI case studies were published in Q1 (up from 232 YoY)

Some clear patterns:

🟩 HR = the dark horse. AI recruiters are being used across the hiring process for efficiency. A notable example is companies that do high-volume hourly hiring (e.g. chain restaurants, retail, and hospitality) using AI to text, screen and book interviews with candidates.
🟩 Sales orgs are going hard on AI, using it to rank the pipeline, score deals, forecast churn, and also using voice agents to qualify leads.
🟩 Legal departments and law firms of every size are now drafting motions, summarizing depositions, and reviewing contracts. Tools like Harvey are blowing up.
🟩 The growth in operations has a lot to do with a specific industry: Healthcare. Every doctor is using AI scribes now (AI that listens to patient visits and drafts the notes).
🟥 Knowledge management: the classic use case of "Enterprise search" aka building one big knowledge base to talk to your docs is plateauing in favour of more specific use cases, like support assistants for employees and synthesising research.
🟥 Customer service is moving away from simple Q&A chatbots (which are very widespread) to more agentic AI that can actually resolve customer requests end-to-end, rather than just be annoying.

The growth story of sales is the clearest: it sits close to the money, so it's an easy way for companies to show ROI on AI implementations — which in turn makes it easier to gain support for other AI initiatives.

HR, though: we all know what's REALLY happening here. Companies need AI recruiters to read 97,465 AI-written applications per day.

And now comes the shameless plug:

Most companies pick & prioritise AI use cases by brainstorming internally + guesswork. And up to 95% of initiatives fail.

What if you could validate your ideas against 2,000+ real implementations with quantified results?

"And then HR said… We’ll get back to you."

For many 2025 MBA graduates, that line is not just a joke anymore — it’s the reality.
The job market has changed.
Companies are hiring — but more selectively.
General MBA profiles are struggling.
Callbacks are slower.
And fresh graduates are stuck refreshing inboxes.

Recent placement insights show:
• Hiring is becoming skill-specific, not degree-specific
• Many non-tech and general MBA roles saw ~15% drop in placements
• Recruiters now prefer analytics, AI, and domain-specialized profiles
• Even top B-schools reported students waiting longer for offers (LinkedIn)

This is not about lack of talent.
It’s about market expectations changing faster than curriculum.

What can 2025 MBA grads do?
→ Add domain skills (Analytics / HR Tech / Finance tools)
→ Build project-based portfolios
→ Network instead of only applying
→ Stay open to internships / contract roles
→ Keep learning while waiting

An MBA is still valuable — but callbacks now go to skills, not just degrees.
If you're a 2025 MBA graduate still waiting…
You're not alone.

Landing in a new market like Malaysia is an adrenaline rush for any MNC. You’ve got the strategy, the budget, and the vision. But then comes the "Real Estate Reality Check."

Traditionally, the move looks like this: sign a 3-year lease, spend 4-5 months navigating contractors, and sink a small fortune into a renovation that you can’t take with you if you scale faster than expected.

In 2026, that’s not just old school—it’s a massive operational risk. Here is why high-growth companies are ditching the "Hammer and Nails" approach for Flexible Private Office Suites.

1. Speed to Market is the New Currency
While your competitors are still arguing with interior designers over floor plans in KL Sentral, you could be fully operational. Flexible spaces allow your team to walk in, plug in, and start hiring on Day 1. No waiting for permits, no WiFi installation delays, and definitely no "renovation dust" on your first board meeting table.

2. The CAPEX vs. OPEX Game
Let’s talk numbers in the context of the Malaysian market today.
For a 2,000 sq. ft. Grade A office in a prime area like TR, KLCC or a formal office tower in Penang, a traditional lease will demand an upfront renovation cost of anywhere between RM 200,000 to RM 500,000 just to make it livable (think RM 100–RM 250 per sq. ft.). That’s capital "frozen" in the walls of a building you don’t own.

Conversely, a flexible private suite requires Zero CAPEX. You pay a higher monthly operational fee, but you keep your half-million Ringgit in the bank to fund your actual business—marketing, talent, and R&D.

3. Avoiding the "Reinstatement Trap"
In Malaysia, most commercial contracts have a "Reinstatement Clause." This means when your lease ends, you have to pay again to tear down those beautiful glass walls and return the space to a "bare shell." It’s effectively paying to build it and paying to destroy it. With a flexible co-working suite, you simply hand back the keys and walk away.

The Real Risks of Traditional Leasing for New Entries:

⛔ The Sunk Cost Risk: If you need to pivot or leave the state within 18 months, that RM 300k renovation is a 100% loss. You can’t liquidate a custom-built reception desk.

⛔ The Focus Risk: Your Country Manager should be focused on market share, not chasing plumbing contractors or managing electricity deposits with TNB.

⛔ The Scalability Risk: Traditional leases are rigid. If you grow from 20 to 50 employees in year one, you’re either stuck in a cramped office or paying massive penalties to break a lease.

For an MNC entering Malaysia, flexibility isn't just about "cool furniture." It’s a strategic hedge against uncertainty. Build your team, build your brand, and let the co-working provider worry about the lightbulbs and the lease terms.

Responsible AI starts with understanding what’s inside the model and that requires both legal and data science at the table

A Cambridge Law paper puts it clearly: AI systems learn on their own, and even their developers don’t always understand the internal logic behind their decisions.

That’s the black box problem.
And the challenge isn’t just technical, it’s a governance challenge.

When a legal team reviews an AI use case, they typically focus on what’s visible: the output, the vendor, the contract terms. 

What’s harder to assess from a purely legal lens is whether the model is learning from biased data, whether it’s amplifying historical patterns, or whether its predictions are confusing correlation with causation.

The paper cites a well-known case: Amazon built an AI tool to screen job applicants. It had to be scrapped after engineers discovered it was systematically downgrading CVs from women — because the model had been trained on historical hiring data dominated by men. The algorithm didn’t discriminate explicitly. It just learned from biased data. Nobody noticed until the damage was done.

That’s not a legal problem. That’s a data problem.

That’s why in Responsible AI governance, the question “does this comply with the regulation?” isn’t enough on its own. 

The question that changes the analysis is: has someone who understands the model validated how it reaches its conclusions?

Legal and Data Science perspectives aren’t competing in AI governance. They’re complementary. Without both at the table, risk assessment stays incomplete.

In a Porter’s Five Forces context, the threat of substitutes for Indian law firms is rising and structurally significant, especially due to technology, alternative service models, and regulatory shifts.

🔁 Threat of Substitutes for Indian Law Firms
1. Legal Tech & AI (High and Growing)
·        Tools for contract drafting, legal research, compliance, and document automation are replacing routine legal work. 
·        Platforms offering DIY legal solutions (templates, automated filings) reduce dependency on lawyers. 
·        AI-driven tools can perform tasks like due diligence, discovery, and research faster and cheaper. 
👉 Impact:
·        Reduces demand for low-value, repetitive legal services 
·        Pushes law firms toward high-value advisory roles 
📌 Evidence: Technology and automated platforms are increasingly substituting traditional services at lower cost 


2. Alternative Legal Service Providers (ALSPs) / LPOs
·        Legal Process Outsourcing (LPO) firms in India offer: 
Contract management 
Document review 
Compliance support 
·        These are cheaper, scalable, and process-driven 
👉 Impact:
·        Corporate clients shift back-office legal work away from law firms 
·        Law firms lose volume-based revenue streams 
📌 Evidence: Growth of LPOs and outsourcing models is a major substitute force 


3. In-house Legal Teams (Strong Substitute)
·        Large corporations are: 
Building robust internal legal departments 
Handling routine advisory, compliance, and contracts internally 
👉 Impact:
·        Law firms are used only for: 
Complex litigation 
Strategic transactions 
·        Reduces repeat billing opportunities 


4. Online Legal Platforms & Marketplaces
·        Platforms offering: 
Fixed-fee legal services 
On-demand lawyer access 
Startup-focused legal packages 
👉 Impact:
·        Price transparency + affordability attracts SMEs/startups 
·        Undercuts traditional hourly billing models 


5. Foreign Law Firms (Emerging Substitute/Hybrid Threat)
·        With gradual liberalization in India: 
Foreign firms can advise on international law, M&A, arbitration 
👉 Impact:
·        High-end corporate clients may prefer: 
Global expertise 
Integrated cross-border services 
📌 Evidence: Entry of foreign firms may reduce referrals and client share for Indian firms 


6. Self-Service & Standardization
·        Increasing use of: 
Templates 
Standard contracts 
Compliance checklists 
👉 Impact:
·        Individuals and startups avoid hiring lawyers for basic needs

I recently joined an insightful cross-industry dialogue in GLOBAL TECH AI ROUNDTABLE.
 It was an insightful discussion with leaders across BFSI, hardware, healthcare, food tech, and IT services, exploring how AI is evolving in each domain and the challenges ahead. 
Hardware leaders emphasized data–compute colocation to reduce GPU costs, while also pointing to latency, memory bandwidth, and token management issues. BFSI highlighted the human–AI synergy with defined roles and responsibilities, alongside regulatory hurdles. Healthcare stressed the need for unified orchestration across various departments like  diagnostics, billing, care divisions etc. Food tech impressed with fast‑track AI adoption through no‑code platforms that empower non‑tech teams to adopt AI. IT services revealed how AI reduces coordination overhead but raises complexities in contract renewals and client expectations.
 
Beyond the industry-specific hurdles, several universal truths emerged that every leader should consider:
·  Clear Shift: AI is moving from technical to business discussions, with 
         mindset gaps spanning decision‑makers, employees, and regulators etc.

·  Approach to AI Solutions: Many still apply deterministic software thinking, 
         but real value emerges when embracing non‑deterministic approaches.

·  Skill Gap: A clear gap exists in AI architecture and system thinking.

·  Cloud vs. On‑prem: Organizations prefer cloud for lightweight solutions, 
         but scale and cost challenges will  drive organization towards 
         hybrid/on‑prem adoption.

·  Team Structure: AI product lifecycles demand new team structures and  
        accountability models (intent lead, context engineer, prompt engineer, 
        system thinker, developer, data owner etc.).

·  AI Explainability: The real challenge lies in trust and workflow design — 
         ensuring solutions are explainable and reliable.

·  Shift in Hiring: Hiring is evolving to prioritize analytical thinkers and system 
        thinkers who can decompose problems and design value‑driven AI 
        solutions.

·  Selling AI Solutions: Selling is complex — beyond outcome‑based pricing, 
         clients demand deterministic cost models, benchmarking, and reliability.

My special thanks to Sohail Khan Ravikanth Chaganti Anupriya Singh Arpit Tandon Sandip Ghosh Hitesh B Jirawla Sandeep Momale Sunjeet Gupta for conducting such excellent session.

I spent 90 days tracking millions of dollars flowing into US, UK and East Asian startups. 

Why? To find where the high-paying remote  jobs appear before they hit job boards.
 
Here’s what I found:
Most lawyers look at job boards.
I looked at funding rounds.
And the pattern was so clear that it changed how I advise every legal professional who wants to work globally.

The 3‑step framework I now use to find legal opportunities before they’re even posted:
1. Follow the “Funding Signal.” 
When a company raises a Series A or B round, they aren’t just “hiring.” 

They are “Aggressively Scaling”

This explosion of capital triggers a tidal wave of complexity.

They need Legal Ops and Compliance in real time to safeguard new investments.

You want to be in their inbox before the job description exists. 

Because by the time the job hits LinkedIn, the first candidate is already in the room.

2. Audit your “Legal Tech Stack” 
Westlaw and LexisNexis are the baseline.
In the global remote market, AI prompt engineering is the new differentiator.

If you can show a CEO how you cut contract review time by 40% using AI, you stop being an expense, you become an asset they fight to keep.

3. Solve “Time‑Zone Anxiety” before they ask.
The biggest fear for a US/UK founder hiring in Africa or Asia isn’t your skill.
It’s the clock.
Don’t wait for them to bring it up.
Lead with: “I am 100% available for EST/GMT work hours – no exceptions.”
Remove the friction before they even feel it. That’s how you win.

Here’s the truth most people need to hear:
The bar isn’t higher for international roles.
The strategy is just different.

You don’t need a new degree.
You need a new roadmap.

I've spent the last several weeks building a Global Legal Career Tracker — and I'm giving it away free.
It's value-packed with:
✅ Startups in the US, UK, and Asia that raised funding in the last 60 days
✅ A smarter system to find hidden remote legal opportunities
✅ A 7-day action plan for applications and interviews
✅ High‑converting outreach scripts for Legal Ops, Compliance, and Paralegal roles
It’s the exact toolkit I’m using right now.

Want a copy?
Like this post
Comment “GUIDE.”
Make sure you’re following me (so I can DM you)

I’ll send the actionable steps directly to your DMs. 📩

Let’s turn your legal training into a global career, one funding round at a time.
